entries.title: Alternator appearance variants — Hitachi vs Mitsubishi (spade terminal)
entries.summary: Replacement alternator may visually differ from the original. Hitachi version has a dummy spade terminal; Mitsubishi version has no spade terminal. Both meet OE specifications.
entries.notes: The dummy/no-spade terminal distinction is functionally important for harness compatibility.

entries.title: Alternator appearance variants — Bosch vs Valeo
entries.summary: Replacement alternator may visually differ from the original. Bosch and Valeo versions both meet OE specifications.

entries.title: Starter appearance variants — Hitachi vs Mitsubishi
entries.summary: Replacement starter may visually differ from the original. Hitachi and Mitsubishi versions both meet OE specifications.
entries.notes: Bilingual (English/French/Spanish).

entries.title: Starter appearance variants — Hitachi 80mm/90mm cases vs Mitsubishi
entries.summary: Replacement starter may differ from the original. Hitachi versions come in 80mm and 90mm case sizes; a Mitsubishi version also exists. All meet OE specifications.
entries.notes: Case size variation is dimensional — verify fit before installation.

entries.title: Starter appearance variants — Valeo vs AC Delco (support bracket note)
entries.summary: Replacement starter may differ from the original. Valeo and AC Delco versions both meet OE specifications. Some AC Delco versions use a support bracket on the back of the starter; shorter versions do not require this bracket.
entries.notes: Bracket presence varies by AC Delco version.

entries.title: Starter appearance variants — Version I vs Version II
entries.summary: Two starter versions exist for this application. Both meet OE specifications.

entries.title: Nissan no-crank — inhibitor relay / starter relay failure
entries.summary: Nissan starting system malfunctions are often misdiagnosed as starter failures. The inhibitor relay (more common failure) or starter relay can produce starter symptoms. Replacing the relay typically resolves the issue.

vehicles.applies_to: Multiple Nissan vehicles equipped with inhibitor and/or starter relay

symptoms: Starter continues running when key is off
entries.cause: Inhibitor relay or starter relay failure, not the starter itself. Both relays prevent the vehicle from starting under certain conditions. Inhibitor relay is the more common failure point.

fixes.location: Right inner fender panel toward front of engine compartment (large blue relay if OE)

fixes.action: Replace starter relay (if Fix #1 did not resolve)

entries.notes: Visual indicator: OE inhibitor relay is large and blue, with markings 25230-89981, 443A, 12V. Located on right inner fender panel.

entries.title: Delco starter shim / no-shim installation
entries.summary: Delco originally used both shim and no-shim starter designs. The replacement starter housing has been pre-measured at the factory; if a shim is required, it is included in the box. Always remove any shim from the failed starter and use only the shim that comes with the replacement (or none if none is included).
warnings: Do NOT reuse the shim from the old starter.
Use ONLY the shim included with the replacement (2mm if included).
If no shim is included, install the starter without a shim.
entries.notes: Shim thickness: 2mm when included. Bulletin covers multiple part numbers; starter appearance may vary from photos.

entries.title: Starter appearance variants — Early vs Late version
entries.summary: Vehicle manufacturer used multiple starter sources for this application. The early and late versions look noticeably different (especially in connector and harness style) but both meet OE specifications.
entries.notes: Connector/harness style differs between early and late versions.

entries.title: Nissan no-crank — inhibitor relay / starter relay failure (duplicate of TBS0054)
entries.summary: Same diagnostic content as TBS0054. Nissan starting system malfunctions are often caused by inhibitor or starter relay failure, not the starter.

vehicles.applies_to: Multiple Nissan vehicles equipped with inhibitor and/or starter relay

symptoms: Starter continues running when key is off
entries.cause: Inhibitor relay or starter relay failure. Inhibitor relay is the more common failure.

fixes.location: Right inner fender panel toward front of engine compartment (large blue relay if OE)

fixes.action: Replace starter relay (if Fix #1 did not resolve)

entries.notes: Content identical to TBS0054, 0077, 0089, 0114. Candidate for de-duplication into a canonical entry.

entries.title: Drive-end armature bushing replacement (VW)
entries.summary: The starter armature is supported by a bushing in the transmission case. A worn bushing can cause symptoms ranging from a slow labored start to a solenoid 'click' with no crank. The bushing must be replaced when installing a new starter on these applications, or warranty is voided.

vehicles.applies_to: Applications where starter armature is supported by transmission-case bushing (likely VW based on visual context)

entries.cause: Worn drive-end armature bushing in the transmission case

fixes.action: Replace drive-end armature bushing in transmission case before installing replacement starter

warnings: MUST replace starter shaft bushing before installing replacement starter — failure to do so voids warranty.
Replacement bushing is temporarily attached to the starter mounting ear for shipping.
entries.notes: Bushing extractor and slide hammer can be rented from equipment rental outlets.

entries.title: Heat shield transfer from failed starter to replacement
entries.summary: Some vehicle applications were originally equipped with a heat shield on the starter solenoid. If the failed starter had a heat shield, it must be transferred to the replacement starter. Many vehicles do not require a heat shield.
warnings: SAVE THE HEAT SHIELD from the failed starter — it must be installed on the replacement.
entries.notes: Heat shield wraps around the starter solenoid.

entries.title: Ford S-terminal harness corrosion — no-crank fix with new harness end
entries.summary: Ford has determined that corrosion in the last 4 inches of the vehicle's S-terminal (ignition) lead can cause a 'no crank' condition. Replacement starters ship with a new vehicle harness end already installed. Cut, strip, and crimp the new connector onto the existing harness 6 inches behind the old connector.

vehicles.applies_to: Ford applications with S-terminal (ignition) starter harness

entries.cause: Corrosion in the last 4 inches of the S-terminal (ignition) lead on the vehicle harness

fixes.action: Cut S-terminal harness 6 inches behind connector, strip 3/8" of insulation, crimp new connector, shrink-tube the splice with heat gun

warnings: Disconnect negative (-) battery cable before starting harness replacement or starter installation.
entries.notes: Hair dryer can substitute for heat gun when shrinking the tubing.

entries.title: Nissan no-crank — inhibitor relay / starter relay failure (duplicate of TBS0054)
entries.summary: Same diagnostic content as TBS0054. Nissan starting system malfunctions are often caused by inhibitor or starter relay failure, not the starter.

vehicles.applies_to: Multiple Nissan vehicles equipped with inhibitor and/or starter relay

symptoms: Starter continues running when key is off
entries.cause: Inhibitor relay or starter relay failure. Inhibitor relay is the more common failure.

fixes.location: Right inner fender panel toward front of engine compartment (large blue relay if OE)

fixes.action: Replace starter relay (if Fix #1 did not resolve)

entries.notes: Content identical to TBS0054, 0060, 0089, 0114.

entries.title: Wiring harness bracket transfer + through-bolt torque
entries.summary: The wiring harness bracket from the failed starter should be transferred to the replacement to secure the harness. When re-installing the bracket, the through-bolt also secures the starter case — torque it correctly to avoid case damage.
warnings: Through bolt secures the starter case as well as the bracket — incorrect torque can damage the case.
Bracket style may vary.
entries.notes: Bracket transfer is optional but recommended for harness security.

entries.title: Nissan no-crank — inhibitor relay / starter relay failure (duplicate of TBS0054)
entries.summary: Same diagnostic content as TBS0054.

vehicles.applies_to: Multiple Nissan vehicles equipped with inhibitor and/or starter relay

symptoms: Starter continues running when key is off
entries.cause: Inhibitor relay or starter relay failure. Inhibitor relay is the more common failure.

fixes.location: Right inner fender panel toward front of engine compartment (large blue relay if OE)

fixes.action: Replace starter relay (if Fix #1 did not resolve)

entries.notes: Content identical to TBS0054, 0060, 0077, 0114.

entries.title: R terminal vs S terminal — connect to correct spade or vehicle won't start
entries.summary: The R terminal relays 12V from one source to another while the ignition key is in start position; not all vehicles use this feature. The S terminal is how the starter receives its start signal. Connecting the vehicle's S harness to the wrong spade terminal will prevent the vehicle from starting.
symptoms: Vehicle will not start (after replacement starter installation)
entries.cause: Vehicle S harness connected to the wrong spade terminal on the replacement starter

fixes.action: Verify S harness is connected to S terminal (not R terminal). Disregard R terminal if vehicle is not equipped to use it.

warnings: If the vehicle S harness is installed to the wrong spade terminal, the vehicle will NOT start.
entries.notes: Common installer error — easy fix once identified.

entries.title: Harness adapter transfer from old starter to replacement
entries.summary: Before returning the failed starter as a core, remove the harness adapter (if equipped) and install it on the replacement starter. Trilingual bulletin (English/French/Spanish).
warnings: Remove harness adapter from old starter before returning as core — install on replacement.

entries.title: Starter appearance variants — Versions I, II, III, IV
entries.summary: Four starter versions exist for this application. All meet OE specifications.
entries.notes: High-variant application — verify fit before installation.

entries.title: Ford enhanced HD starter replacement — terminal A/B wiring change
entries.summary: This enhanced HD starter replaces early-style Ford starters with a remote-mounted solenoid. The new design is more compact, more dependable, and produces more torque with less amp draw. Installation requires re-routing the starter cable from terminal B to terminal A and adding the included wire harness to terminal B.

vehicles.applies_to: Early-style Ford starters with remote-mounted solenoid

warnings: Move starter cable from terminal B to terminal A.
Install included wire harness to terminal B.
Re-secure attaching nuts on terminals A and B.
All other connections remain the same.
entries.notes: Benefits: more dependable, more compact, more torque with less amp draw.

entries.title: Starter appearance variants — Bosch vs Delco
entries.summary: Replacement starter may differ from the original. Bosch and Delco versions both meet OE specifications.

entries.title: GM crankshaft position sensor failure causing starter/flywheel damage (DTC P0338)
entries.summary: Hard start symptoms and starter failure on GM applications may be caused by crankshaft position sensor failure, not the starter. A failing CKP sensor allows up to 50 extra degrees of spark advance during cranking, creating high cylinder pressure and 'kickback' that can damage the starter housing, flywheel/flexplate, and engine block at the starter boss. Replacing the starter without fixing the root cause will result in repeat damage.

vehicles.applies_to: GM applications with crankshaft position sensor — see GM TSB 00-06-04-014

symptoms: Grinding or unusual noises during cranking or start-up
Cracked or broken engine block at the starter boss
Broken starter ring gear on flywheel or flexplate
entries.cause: Crankshaft position sensor failure causing up to 50 extra degrees of spark advance during cranking, leading to kickback that damages starter, flywheel/flexplate, and engine block

fixes.action: Inspect for DTC P0338. If present, replace crankshaft position sensor and inspect for damage at starter boss, drive housing, and flywheel ring gear.

warnings: Failure to replace a failed crankshaft position sensor will result in repeated damage to the starter or flywheel.
Mild flywheel wear is normal; missing, broken, or chipped teeth are NOT normal.
entries.notes: DTC P0338 = Crankshaft Position Sensor 'A' Circuit High Input. Cross-reference GM TSB 00-06-04-014.

entries.title: Nissan no-crank — inhibitor relay / starter relay failure (duplicate of TBS0054)
entries.summary: Same diagnostic content as TBS0054.

vehicles.applies_to: Multiple Nissan vehicles equipped with inhibitor and/or starter relay

symptoms: Starter continues running when key is off
entries.cause: Inhibitor relay or starter relay failure. Inhibitor relay is the more common failure.

fixes.location: Right inner fender panel toward front of engine compartment (large blue relay if OE)

fixes.action: Replace starter relay (if Fix #1 did not resolve)

entries.notes: Content identical to TBS0054, 0060, 0077, 0089.

entries.title: Starter shimming procedure — 1/8 inch drill bit gap test
entries.summary: Always start by re-using existing shims from the old starter. If engagement is noisy, use a 1/8 inch drill bit to measure the gap between the top of a flywheel tooth and the starter armature shaft (through the inspection cover). Snug fit = correctly shimmed. Loose = remove a shim. Too tight or won't fit = add a shim.
entries.cause: Incorrect shim thickness causing wrong starter-to-flywheel clearance

fixes.action: Measure gap with 1/8" drill bit through inspection cover; adjust shims based on fit (snug=correct, loose=remove shim, tight=add shim).

warnings: If starter cannot be correctly shimmed or remains noisy after correct shimming, contact MPA Technical Service at 800-228-9672.
entries.notes: 1/8" drill bit serves as a quick gauge for correct flywheel/armature clearance.

entries.title: Starter appearance variants — Version I vs Version II
entries.summary: Two starter versions exist for this application. Both meet OE specifications.

entries.title: Starter appearance variants — Version I vs Version II
entries.summary: Two starter versions exist for this application. Both meet OE specifications.

entries.title: Nissan cold-start no-crank — IPDM (Intelligent Power Distribution Module) failure
entries.summary: Specific Nissan no-crank condition that occurs only after the vehicle has not been started for several hours in cold weather (below 10°F / 12°C), then driven briefly (5-10 minutes), then turned off briefly (5-10 minutes). If all three conditions match, the IPDM (Intelligent Power Distribution Module) likely needs replacement. Includes part numbers for 11 Nissan models.
symptoms: Starter no crank ONLY when ALL of the following conditions occur: vehicle not started for several hours, outside temperature below 10°F (12°C), vehicle driven briefly (5-10 min), engine off briefly (5-10 min), then no-crank
entries.cause: IPDM (Intelligent Power Distribution Module) failure under specific cold-start cycling conditions
warnings: Bulletin only applies if ALL listed cold-cycling conditions are met. Other no-crank causes should still be diagnosed normally.
entries.notes: IPDM = Intelligent Power Distribution Module. Refer to vehicle service manual for replacement instructions and IPDM location. Available through Nissan dealerships.

entries.title: Starter appearance variants — 8-tooth and 11-tooth versions
entries.summary: Three starter versions exist for this application: one 8-tooth pinion and two 11-tooth pinion variants. All meet OE specifications. CRITICAL: pinion tooth count must match the flywheel ring gear.
warnings: Verify pinion tooth count matches the application before installation.
entries.notes: Tooth count is functionally critical, not just cosmetic — verify against application data.

entries.title: Starter appearance variants — Version I vs Version II
entries.summary: Two starter versions exist for this application. Both meet OE specifications.

entries.title: Ford S-terminal harness corrosion — no-crank fix (similar to TBS0073)
entries.summary: Ford has determined that corrosion in the last 4 inches of the vehicle's S-terminal (ignition) lead can cause a 'no crank' condition. Replacement starters ship with a new vehicle harness end already installed. Cut, strip, and crimp the new connector onto the existing harness.

vehicles.applies_to: Ford applications with S-terminal (ignition) starter harness

entries.cause: Corrosion in the last 4 inches of the S-terminal (ignition) lead on the vehicle harness

fixes.action: Cut S-terminal harness 6 inches behind connector, strip 3/8" of insulation, crimp new connector onto stripped wire.

entries.notes: Updated version of TBS0073 with two starter appearance variants shown.
